speaker: interviewer
text: This is Kim Feinberg. I'm interviewing Motel Pogir in Johannesburg, South Africa. 1995 the 11th of November. This is tape number five.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, so we-- we were on the fifth-- on the sixth time. Five times we were caught and returned back to the border and sitting in no-man's land when the police used to walk away, we used to go back to Italy. And on the sixth time now, I become friendly [? during all these ?] [INAUDIBLE] with a police ex-officer, a Jewish boy. I became friendly with him, and we decided that when they will drive us, they will catch us. If they catch us this time, I will fall one side of the road and you will fall the other side of the road, and the group will drive away. We know already where to go, and we'll go ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who was guiding you? Did you have special guides?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had guides, yes. Israeli. Israelis, yes. So-- and so it was. We were caught again, and it was 3 o'clock in the morning. And he fell one side of the road, I fell the other side of the road. On the border, there is high grass, quite high grass. So we lay in the grass. The group had driven away. And we then crawled through on our knees. We crawled about two kilometers. We crawled through the danger zone. And we went up to a house. We didn't know what house it is. And we knocked at the door on the house. And they let us in. And who was it but a German woman. A German woman. She was very friendly, and we told her that we are Jewish, we escaped from there and there and we are Jewish and we can't get through the border and please help us out, and she agreed to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she said to us that she's not going to let us stay in the house, but she will let us stay outside in the stables. Do you remember her name, Motel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And she was really nice to us. She gave us-- we paid her for it. We had money. So then she-- she organized for us a taxi. And the old Fords, they big like a box. The old Fords. That driver from the taxi he have the permit also to go through the roadblocks, and from there were 60 kilometers we were away from the railway station. And the 60 kilometers was four roadblocks. And by putting out his ticket, they didn't search us, and we went into the roadblock, the two of us. And we went up to a station. In Italy, we already and we come up and we ask Bileter. They told us to ask Bileter, the Milano, and we met Bileter in Milano. We came to Milano. Then we met up with our wives, with the kids in Milano.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where to go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were taken. We didn't know. Where would we know to go in Milano? Milano is a big place. And in Milano, we stayed nine months until we applied for South Africa to come in. They didn't let us in twice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing there during that nine months?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you that I register myself and became a registered electrician. So I was looking after the electrical. And then I had enough from the electrical, because it was always trouble. I resigned from being electrical. One day they used to overload the power, you see. And I had a lot of trouble, so I resigned from it. And I became a baker, because we come from a bakery. So I used to work in a bakery from 4:00 in the morning until to 3:00 in the afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in this camp?

speaker: interviewer
text: And most of them were from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of them were from Lithuania, Latvia, Estonia, Germany, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Poland. From everywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your wife reached this camp, was your little boy there already?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and she had trouble to get him back because she's dark and he's blond. So they said to him that this-- they said to my wife that she is not his mother. So she said to him, all right, bring him out and let me see him and he'll see me, and if he won't recognize me, then it's not my son. But just bring him out. Once he was brought out before the mother, he start to cry, Mommy. Mommy. Mommy. He fell on Mommy's neck and that was cry all over the place. Mommy, mommy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was life like in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: The camp was quite nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep, Motel?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a little-- with blankets we partitioned up our beds. We had a little bed and a little table. One little bed, three-foot bed, and the kids were sleeping somewhere else. And it was quite-- quite-- quite a good time we had in Italy. We went to the opera, We walked. We used to go around free in town and anywhere. We used to go to [INAUDIBLE], and we had money. We enjoyed ourselves in there. And then we apply to Rhodesia. Rhodesia let us in straightaway.

speaker: interviewer
text: But now why did you apply to Rhodesia when the whole point of this was to go to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, They also told us being in the camp, in Milano in the camp they told us they are only letting in people who can jump offshore. Because the British government are not letting in boats deep into there, so we had to jump offshore in the sea, the people to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were-- we had Selwin with us, a little baby, so with babies they didn't let us in. They didn't let us take him in to go to Israel. So we remained. What could we do? We have a brother in South Africa. They knocked us back. We had a brother in Rhodesia. They let us in, in Rhodesia. What was the reason that they knocked you back from South Africa? Why didn't they accept you?

words.text: Africa? Why didn't they accept you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no reason given. We were told that the Jan Smuts government didn't like any Jews to let them into the country after that. Whether true or not, I can't say. That was the more or less saying that. And we had in Rhodesia I started to work 10 days later for a firm, Hoover Davis, a big firm. I can carry on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Electrical, electrician. I'm a qualified electrician. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they sacked me after a week because the reason was in Hoover Davis organization there is no Jewish working. So they sacked me. And I got a job with another English firm, and I worked there until I learned a bit of English. 14 months I worked there until I learned a little English to fill in a schedule for the council for the electrical, and I got registered with the industrial council, electrical. And I worked-- and I was then joined the Master Builders Association. And we did very well as electricians. I used to have big, big jobs. And the Bulawayo community supported me high hearted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now Motel, tell me, when you arrived in Rhodesia, did you know anyone there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I had a brother there. My brother came to fetch me from Beira. We came by boat from Beira. The UNRA, I still got the tickets from the United Nations. They paid for us for our fare.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your first reaction arriving there?

speaker: interviewee
text: That they-- [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] when they served us in the morning, they serve us, we arrived in the morning, so there was white bread on the table. So I said to Ida, my brother must be a rich man. White bread in the morning. That was-- and I started to work and I was very successful in Bulawayo. And I built one block of flats and then another block of flats and a house for ourselves and another property. And I was doing bigger electrical installations.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your brother do then, Motel?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a builder in the building industry, so I had all his work. And I had big, big like [? spring ?] master builder, and I make a factory. I did electrical installation. And there's a firm by JH Plane, [? it calls a firm, ?] a Christian firm, and through the architect I got there a job, a very big job and some other six high-floor buildings. I did quite a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other children during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In '48, Ruben was born. You'll see him just now.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in Bulawayo?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived in Bulawayo the end of '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you have two sons.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What school did they go to there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Milton High School in Bulawayo. Milton High School. And then they came here after they finished Milton High School and they went to university here. They settled themselves and they didn't want to come back. They got married. They got settled themselves and then they asked us to come, we should come to them, and we came.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in South Africa?

speaker: interviewee
text: In September of '48-- sorry, '78.

speaker: interviewer
text: Motel, looking back at all these experiences, how do you feel now when you sit and you speak about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you. While I'm speaking, I don't feel really anything. Why I'm only serious, I can't believe that I went through all this while I'm speaking. And always-- it comes always through my mind that it's with luck, not really I'm sorry to say that with luck, a bit of luck, we went through all that trouble. A bit of luck. And the main thing was I was a big chance, take big, big chances. Because to me, life was worth nothing. I knew that we're not going to live through, so take a chance. Take a chance. And by taking these chances, it worked. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And today I'm 81. I'm retired. But since I arrived here in Johannesburg I am still doing electrical, electrical. I've got in my ledger 165 names from people who phone me for jobs. In fact, I've got for Monday a job also from one of them. And this week I had three jobs and I'm quite busy. I'm not taking on any more big work, big jobs. But I'm self-repair things, [INAUDIBLE] and lights and this and that. And believe it or not, two weeks ago I put on a roof for somebody. They bought a house-- a townhouse, and it was a little yard enclosed, and they wanted to close in. They called me if I can put on a roof, and I put a roof on because I was in the building industry. And they were very happy and I was happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Motel, when you were bringing up your children, did you tell them of your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, many times I sit with them and they know all these experiences what I went through, they know that. Whether they remember I can't tell you. I'm sure they should. But you'll see them just now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you bring them up in a Jewish home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, completely Jewish home. And they still Ruben is an active shul goer in Sandton. He lives in Sandton. And Selwin is in [INAUDIBLE], in shul. And in fact, they hang a-- a-- a certificate for me getting last year in the shul for being an honorable member. There's the certificate hanging there. You've seen it. And this year also they write to use we should come, they are going to give us a certificate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Motel, how do you feel about the German people now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't feel any-- I don't feel-- Generally, I don't like them, generally. But hatred, I haven't got any hatred for them. Real hatred, that only there were a handful of dictators and they brought in-- they brought in all these bad laws for the Jewish people. Dictatorship. They just dictated-- and the nation, I suppose, believed them. I'm not going to talk for them, but I-- I'm not the type of person which I keep hatred in my heart for anybody. I can have a fight with you today, and tomorrow we can shake hands again, forget about what it was. Life goes on.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you could give a message to the world and to your children and to your grandchildren, what would that message be?

speaker: interviewee
text: The message would be that hopefully it won't-- it won't come such a bad time for our Jewish nation in the whole of the world. We lived through enough. We lost six million of our best people. We lost through Hitler's being such a Nazi, such a terrible man where he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just wish them that they should be united. Can I speak? They should be-- the Israelis, the Yiddishfolk, Yiddish people should be united between themselves and they will go through all that big problems which it comes, if it comes any problems to them. They must be united. Like I wish that Israel would be united now and having all these troubles with shooting and killing and so on. I'm really upset about these sort of things.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm a good friend of Israel. I had two sisters, other sisters, in Israel, which we used to go to give [INAUDIBLE] One is still alive and we are still in contact. I still get love letters from her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now Motel, looking back, just to close this testimony. Looking back on your life, what would you say is important to you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, important to me would be if my wife would've come back to normality, but it is impossible. And I only wish my family to be united together and not to have any arguments between each other for money or something like this. Be a united mishpocheh and you'll go through all the problems, whenever it arises in the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Motel, one final, last word. How do you feel now that you've done this testimony?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you, I feel quite relieved. Talking that whole thing through, my life story through, which I haven't done it so much. I haven't done it so much. It's the first time I'm doing it, and I hope the last. And because I have written down, I've got written down all my biography. I've got it written down in Yiddish and in English and typed. I've got about 20 pages. And we carrying on in that respect.

speaker: interviewer
text: Motel, thank you so much for doing this. We so appreciate your input to this project.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thank you very much for coming to me, and I appreciate very, very much for me getting a chance to come on onto these tapes, which I hope it will be successful. And I hope to get a photostat of the copy. Thank you, both of you, very much. This is me, Motel, in a photo taken in 1935 in the Lithuanian cavalry uniform. [PAUSES FOR 29 SECONDS] This is my sister Fagah. My other sister Tabar with her husband, Chaim. [SPEAKING YIDDISH] I said it in Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to repeat it in English, Motel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, please. All right, I'll repeat it in English. Doesn't matter. OK. Hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: In Yiddish. English. All right. I'll repeat it in English. Shall I start? This is my sisters, two sisters, Tabar and Fagelah. And her husband. Taken in 1937, '38. I cannot say the date exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wiped out. Wiped out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear him saying that? OK. That looks so great. I love that.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my older brother, Chaim, who was killed in a Russian unit when the Germans retreated from Russia in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is his wife, Bessa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was killed by the Germans. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] This is my sister Fagah on the left. My late brother Simon. That's myself in the middle. My late brother Chaim, My late sister Tobe. In the middle are my mom and dad. [PAUSES FOR 39 SECONDS] This is my wife, Ida. She is four years in the old-age home now with Alzheimer's disease. [PAUSES FOR 43 SECONDS] That's myself, Motel Pogir. This picture on my motorbike, which I bought that motorbike for four liters of vodka from a Russian officer in 1945. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Photographed at the stores where I was manager. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] You know, I had a motorbike before the war, but the Russians confiscated it from me when the war started. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is me, Motel Pogir, taking the photo in 19-- in 1938 in Kaunas, in Lithuania. [PAUSES FOR 34 SECONDS] This is Ida, my wife, and Motel Pogir, in the year 1968. Taken in Bulowayo after winning the [INAUDIBLE] Club. [PAUSES FOR 34 SECONDS] This is my wife, Ida Pogir. Taken in Bulawayo in 1979. [PAUSES FOR 21 SECONDS] This is my grandson, Craig. He is now in Israel on [INAUDIBLE]. This is Ruben's and Sandra's younger son. [PAUSES FOR 30 SECONDS] This is my grandson, Byron. He is in America now. This is Selwin's older son and my eldest grandson. [PAUSES FOR 16 SECONDS] This is my oldest grandson, Byron, and Lauren, daughter. Selwin's two kids. My older son Selwin's two kids. [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS] These little shoes are left from the memory of my little darling son Henich, who was killed by the Nazis in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave you these boots?

speaker: interviewee
text: The farmer from he was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the farmer where he was hidden gave you these boots.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING YIDDISH] [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] [? I know. ?] [? What are you going to do? ?]

words.text: [? What are you going to do? ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was Henich when he was killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: 2 and 1/2. [PAUSES FOR 40 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: On my left is our son, Selwin. On my right is our son, Ruben. Behind me is Joy, Selwin's darling. And behind Ruben is Sandra, his darling. Our grandchildren, from left to right, is Richard, Selwin's and Joy's son. In the middle is Larry, Ruben's and Sandra's son. And on the right from Larry is Darren, Sandra's and Ruben's son, the older one.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: I was actually born in Lithuania and grew up basically not to really being able to remember anything for the first couple years of my life. And as I matured and we basically grew up in Bulawayo in then Rhodesia, I listened to both Motel and Ida tell me the stories of what happened in Lithuania before and during the war. And this was something that I lived with on a daily basis. It became second nature. And it was not unexpected for me to come home and there be friends of any one of the members of the family and be told a very interesting story. Motel was fantastic in relating all of these stories. And it was really unbelievable listening to the survival issue and the hardships that were experienced. I just think that now thinking back it was just phenomenal that Motel and Ida managed to really survive and get through it and be able to be sitting here and relating the story. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We-- we really are very proud and fantastic to have lived and listened to all these stories. Unfortunately, Ida's not here with us. She suffers from Alzheimer's. She's in a home. That is another test that Motel has had to live with at this point in time. And he must be [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] probably the strongest guy I know. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately. [SIGHING] [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: You can't speak? Cool off.

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm the emotional one in the family, as you can probably see. And it's very difficult for me to relate and tell you how I feel. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I just relate some stories of my youth that I remember? Some of the interesting things that happened is-- is growing up in Africa, in Bulawayo specifically, I arrived as a young person. I could only speak Yiddish. I couldn't speak English. And this in itself set me apart from the other kids. And children, as a rule, are cruel. And because I was different, I was picked on. And I'm sure that I had something inbuilt which gave me absolute strength to survive. And we get became known as a brawler and around every corner I was ready and willing to-- to basically sort out any problems, particularly when things were pointed at any of the Jewish kids or any of the Jewish people or any of the actual antisemitic remarks that were made. I was always-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they-- they came to fetch me and-- and got me to come and sort it out. Thinking back, it's not the proudest thing that I've done, but it was a message, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a message basically to the non-Jews [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to basically realize that we can survive. [CRYING] And here we are. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, OK, I can only say that it's been quite an experience listening to Motel and Ida over many years. We've learnt a lot. We are a small family. We're a very close family. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And I'm proud that Motel has had the opportunity to put on tape his experiences, that people around the world can maybe learn from this-- these experiences and the hardships that these guys went through. And to think that this guy's 81, and he's absolutely unbelievable. He still does his odd jobs. He's still got the best sense of humor. Still plays his sport. Really lives his full life at the moment and even with the Ida-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, cut.
